input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A 1/10-scale teleoperated autonomous vehicle retrofitted with advanced sensor and control technology, featuring lower and upper chassis layers and autonomy elements. Operates within the Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) domain as part of Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS), with resource-constrained electronic control units (ECUs) necessitating computational offloading and low-latency communication for safety-critical operations.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: Three core services: 1) Data Monitoring via a user interface (UI) for real-time visualization of vehicle state data, projected driving path, and detected object tracks, with capabilities to save and generate reports; 2) Computation Offloading of the ACC system (lead vehicle detection, speed computation based on distance, and actuation commands) to cloud-based resources to alleviate on-board ECU constraints; 3) Remote Management allowing operators to enable/disable ACC, set desired speed, and send emergency braking commands through the UI. Target users include operators for real-time monitoring and management, and developers for troubleshooting using collected data. The system adheres to the Fully Autonomous Digital Twin with Human-in-the-Loop taxonomy.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The Digital Twin system employs NVIDIA Jetson NX and Jetson Orin Nano hardware for edge computing, Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR for sensor data acquisition, Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2) as the middleware framework, NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 for GPU acceleration, Python and C++ for application development, MATLAB/Simulink for modeling and simulation, and complies with ISO 23247 architectural standards for digital twin systems.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Clock synchronization requirement of 100 ms accuracy for computation offloading in safety-critical vehicular applications, mandated by industry standards and constrained by hardware limitations.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: Entity-based architecture with three distinct entities (User Entity, Digital Twin Entity, Physical Twin Entity) each deployed on separate hardware components; each Functional Entity (FE) implemented as individual ROS2 packages containing ROS2 nodes, with User Interface FE split into 3D Visualization and Control Panel subcomponents, and Physical Twin Entity deviating from ISO 23247 by hosting general automotive functionality such as Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: ISO 23247 Reference Architecture (RA) used for design, mapping software components to Functional Entities (FEs) implemented as ROS2 packages on vehicle hardware; entity structure includes User Entity (3D Visualization, Control Panel), Digital Twin Entity (data management, control mechanisms), Physical Twin Entity (sensors, actuators, Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking), each on separate hardware.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin system employs Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking models that process raw LiDAR point clouds using DBSCAN clustering followed by Kalman filter tracking. The Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) model determines lead vehicle position and computes following speed. Data management utilizes a SQL database for storing object tracks and ego vehicle state, with Report Generation components for visualization and analysis.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Physical acting components include a Vedder Electronic Speed Controller interfacing with a DC motor for propulsion control and a steering servo for directional adjustment. The Actuation software converts desired speed commands into precise motor voltage signals to control vehicle dynamics.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensing infrastructure consists of a Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR and an Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera, as documented in Section III.B of the source materials.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Raw LiDAR point cloud data is processed by the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking software component, converting raw 2D plane points into structured track data. Vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are transmitted in real-time via ROS2 for intra-component communication and UDP over Wi-Fi for inter-entity communication. The Data Translation component serializes all data streams, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: ACC-related control commands for motor and steering actuators are processed through the ACC Controlling FE and Actuation FE components. Data Translation serializes control signals, aligns message formats, and performs unit conversions before transmission via ROS2 for internal component communication and UDP over Wi-Fi for inter-entity communication.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Internal communication between software components uses ROS2, while inter-entity communication occurs over Wi-Fi. Requirement R7 specifies a maximum network latency of 100 ms. Bandwidth requirements, redundancy mechanisms, and security protocols are not explicitly detailed in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: Each entity is deployed on separate hardware components, including NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano. Data storage is maintained on a device external to the vehicle as per Requirement R3. Computational requirements, scalability mechanisms, and deployment models are not explicitly detailed beyond these specifics.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering process includes Requirements gathering, Architectural design phase, and Implementation of Digital Twin Services (DTS) such as Data Monitoring (real-time sensor data collection and visualization), Computation Offloading (analytics processing via distributed systems), and Remote Management (user interfaces for issuing control commands and receiving reports).
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Decision support capabilities are provided through Data Monitoring for real-time sensor data insights, Computation Offloading for analytics processing by offloading tasks to cloud/edge resources, and Remote Management for user interaction via command interfaces and report generation, as specified in Section III.A and IV.A.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The ISO 23247 standard for Digital Twin framework in manufacturing specifies a reference architecture in Part 2, consisting of Observable Manufacturing Element (OME), Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and User Entity. Functional Entities (FEs) within this architecture may implement OPC Unified Architecture for data collection and device control.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extracted characteristic accurately describes the physical twin as a 1/10-scale teleoperated autonomous vehicle retrofitted with advanced sensors, specific chassis layers (lower and upper), and autonomy elements. It correctly contextualizes the system within the ACC domain of ADAS and highlights the necessity for computational offloading due to resource-constrained ECUs. This is strongly supported by the text: 'developing an Adaptive Cruise Control DT for a 1/10-scale autonomous vehicle', 'teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced sensor and control technology', 'lower level chassis... upper level chassis', and 'resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient'.

The description correctly identifies the three core services provided by the Digital Twin: Data Monitoring (via UI for vehicle state, path, and tracks), Computation Offloading (moving ACC processing to the cloud), and Remote Management (ACC enable/disable, speed settings, braking). It also accurately lists target users (operators and developers) and the specific taxonomy classification. Evidence supports this: 'Data Monitoring: ... user interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate reports... projected driving path, and detected object tracks', 'Computation Offloading: ... offloading only the ACC system from the vehicle to the DT', 'Remote Management: ... enabling/disabling ACC, setting the desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command', and 'operators... developers... Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop'.

The description accurately enumerates the specific hardware and software tools mentioned in the text. It correctly lists the NVIDIA Jetson NX and Orin Nano, Hokuyo LiDAR, ROS2 middleware, Jetpack 5.0 SDK, and programming languages Python and C++. It also includes MATLAB/Simulink and the ISO 23247 architectural standard. The extraction is strongly grounded in the provided text, referencing the hardware layers and development stack explicitly. Supporting quotes: 'NVIDIA Jetson NX antenna and NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano microcomputer', 'Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR', 'Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2) ... as the middleware', and 'investigate the fitness of the ISO 23247 reference architecture for automotive DTs'.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction correctly identifies the 100 ms accuracy requirement and its context in computation offloading and safety-critical applications. It accurately captures the constraints mentioned in the text, though the phrasing 'mandated by industry standards and constrained by hardware limitations' combines the justification for latency (R7, mandated by 3GPP) and clock synchronization (R8, constrained by hardware) into a single sentence which is slightly imprecise but factually grounded. Quotes: 'Components within the DTS shall have clocks synchronized to an accuracy of 100 ms.', 'To enable computation offloading...', 'We require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.'

The extraction provides a highly specific and detailed description of the entity-based architecture, the separation of hardware, the ROS2 implementation strategy, and the specific deviation from ISO 23247 regarding the Physical Twin Entity. It comprehensively covers the multiplicities and structural aspects. Quotes: 'User Entity... Digital Twin Entity... Physical Twin Entity', 'each entity has been deployed on a different hardware component', 'Physical Twin Entity reflects the inherent resources... and can host general automotive functionality, such as Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking.'

The extraction accurately describes the overall architecture based on ISO 23247, the mapping to Functional Entities (FEs), and the breakdown of the User, Digital Twin, and Physical Twin entities. It correctly identifies the use of ROS2 packages. However, it incorrectly states that packages are implemented 'on vehicle hardware' when the text specifies that entities are deployed on 'different hardware components' (implying some are external). This slight inaccuracy prevents a perfect score. Quotes: 'We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design phase.', 'implement each FE as an individual ROS2 package.', 'each entity has been deployed on a different hardware component.'

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The provided text describes the internal architecture of the Digital Twin System (User, DT, Physical Twin entities) and their internal communication protocols (ROS2, Wi-Fi). It does not describe integration with external enterprise systems, other digital twins, fleet management systems, or specific interoperability standards for horizontal exchange. Quotes regarding internal architecture (e.g., 'The DTS shall use a standardized communication interface between all components of the system') do not describe horizontal integration with outside systems.

block_3_docs_retrieved:
In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately details the computational models and data management. It correctly identifies the 'Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking' models (DBSCAN, Kalman filter), the ACC model's logic, and the SQL database usage found in Table I and the text.

The extraction correctly identifies the 'Vedder Electronic Speed Controller' and the 'Actuation' software logic described in Table I. However, it adds unsupported details like 'DC motor' and 'steering servo', which are not explicitly named in the source text, though the steering control function is mentioned.
The extraction precisely lists the 'Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR' and 'Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera' as specified in Section III.B of the source text.

While the introduction defines Digital Twins generally as 'real-time and high-fidelity computational reflections', the document does not provide specific details regarding model fidelity requirements, validation and verification methods, uncertainty sources, or error detection mechanisms for the specific ACC system being engineered. The requirements section focuses on network latency and clock synchronization rather than model fidelity or validation processes. Consequently, the specific technical details required by this characteristic are absent.

Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a. Raw point cloud data from the LiDAR sensor must be processed by the _Point Cloud_ _Clustering & Tracking_ SW component, which turns the raw point cloud, represented as individual points on a 2D plane,

block_4_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately describes the processing of LiDAR data by the 'Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking' component and the role of the Data Translation component in serializing data and performing unit conversions, as seen in Table I and Section IV-C. It correctly identifies ROS2 for internal communication. However, the extraction states 'UDP over Wi-Fi' for inter-entity communication, whereas the provided source text cuts off at 'using U', making 'UDP' technically unsupported by the provided evidence. Additionally, the inclusion of 'projected driving path' in the transmission list is an inference from display requirements (R4) rather than an explicit data stream description.

The extraction correctly identifies the flow of control commands through the 'ACC Controlling FE' and 'Actuation FE' components and accurately describes the Data Translation component's functions ('serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions'). It correctly cites ROS2 for internal communication. Similar to the previous characteristic, it claims 'UDP over Wi-Fi' for inter-entity communication, which is not fully supported by the provided text that ends at 'using U'.

The extraction correctly identifies the internal (ROS2) and external (Wi-Fi) communication protocols and the specific latency requirement (R7). It also accurately identifies that bandwidth, redundancy, and security details are absent from the text. Supported by quotes: 'R7. The DTS shall maintain network latency of at most 100 ms' and 'inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network'.

The extraction correctly identifies the deployment of entities on separate hardware, specifically mentioning the NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano, and the external data storage requirement (R3). It correctly notes the absence of detailed computational requirements and scalability models in the text. Supported by quotes: 'each entity has been deployed on a different hardware component', 'NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano', and 'R3. The DTS shall store the ego vehicle state data on a device external to the vehicle'.

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

block_5_judge.reasoning: The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The source text mentions 'design phase' and 'operation phase' of automotive systems in general, and describes the specific engineering process of the case study (Requirements, Architecture, Implementation). However, it does not define the lifecycle stages, transition management, or lifecycle governance of the Digital Twin entity itself as required by the characteristic definition. Thus, the extraction is correct.

The extraction accurately summarizes the engineering steps defined in Section IV (Requirements, Architectural design, Implementation) and the three main Digital Twin Services (Data Monitoring, Computation Offloading, Remote Management) described in Section III.A. However, it describes the ACC computation as 'analytics processing', while the text specifically refers to it as 'computes a desired speed' or 'computation', making 'analytics' a slight interpretive stretch.

The extraction correctly identifies the decision support capabilities provided by the three services (Data Monitoring, Computation Offloading, Remote Management) and their functions (real-time insights, processing, user interaction) as per Sections III.A and IV.A. It slightly over-specifies the implementation details by mentioning 'cloud/edge resources' where the text only explicitly mentions 'cloud' or 'better-suited equipment', and refers to the processing as 'analytics' rather than 'computation' or 'control'.

The extraction provides a highly specific and accurate summary of the ISO 23247 standard found in Section II.A. It correctly identifies Part 2's reference architecture, lists the four entities (OME, Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity, User Entity), and mentions the use of OPC UA for Functional Entities as an example implementation.

block_6_judge.reasoning: The source documents focus on the architectural design, components, and services of the Digital Twin, such as 'data monitoring', 'computation offloading', and 'remote management'. They discuss storing vehicle state data externally but do not contain any information regarding data governance, ownership policies, privacy protection, consent management, or anonymization. Therefore, 'Not in Document' is correct.

While the text mentions the 'safety-critical nature of the system' and the implementation of the 'ISO 15622' standard for ACC functionality, it does not describe specific security and safety measures as defined by the characteristic. There are no details on 'cybersecurity protocols, access control mechanisms, authentication systems, threat mitigation strategies, fail-safe mechanisms, or risk assessment approaches'. Thus, the specific technical details required are not present, making 'Not in Document' accurate.
